title: Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied

infobox.value: Inhabitants | of which Foreigners | Area in ha. | Inhabitants per ha. | Source

(German pronunciation: [[ˈaʊbɪŋ lɔxˈhaʊzn̩ ˈlaŋviːt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Aubing-Lochhausn-Langwied*) is the 22nd borough of the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). The districts **Aubing**, **Lochhausen** and **Langwied** were incorporated together in 1942, thus forming the largest borough of the city at 34,06 km2, yet having the lowest density of population with 37,352 residents as of 2002. This large borough still accommodates many vast agricultural and forest areas that together with the [Aubinger Lohe](/w/index.php?title=Aubinger_Lohe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aubinger Lohe (page does not exist)") and the [Langwieder lake district](/wiki/Langwieder_lake_district "Langwieder lake district"), fulfill the [balance of nature](/wiki/Balance_of_nature "Balance of nature") in the area and offer recreational facilities to all of western Munich.

[Coordinates](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"): [48°09′30″N 11°25′10″E﻿ / ﻿48.15833°N 11.41944°E﻿ / 48.15833; 11.41944](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&params=48_09_30_N_11_25_10_E_region:DE-BY_type:city_source:dewiki)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Borough of Munich, Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_22_%28Karte%29_-_Aubing_-_Lochhausen_-_Langwied.png/250px-M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_22_%28Karte%29_-_Aubing_-_Lochhausen_-_Langwied.png)](/wiki/File:M%C3%BCnchen_-_Stadtbezirk_22_(Karte)_-_Aubing_-_Lochhausen_-_Langwied.png)

Location of borough 22 in Munich

**Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied** (German pronunciation: [[ˈaʊbɪŋ lɔxˈhaʊzn̩ ˈlaŋviːt]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian"): *Aubing-Lochhausn-Langwied*) is the 22nd borough of the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") city of [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). The districts **Aubing**, **Lochhausen** and **Langwied** were incorporated together in 1942, thus forming the largest borough of the city at 34,06 km2, yet having the lowest density of population with 37,352 residents as of 2002. This large borough still accommodates many vast agricultural and forest areas that together with the [Aubinger Lohe](/w/index.php?title=Aubinger_Lohe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aubinger Lohe (page does not exist)") and the [Langwieder lake district](/wiki/Langwieder_lake_district "Langwieder lake district"), fulfill the [balance of nature](/wiki/Balance_of_nature "Balance of nature") in the area and offer recreational facilities to all of western Munich.

A large area between Aubing/Neuaubing and [Germering](/wiki/Germering "Germering") on Munich ground of the [Freiham](/wiki/Freiham "Freiham") district, east of *Gut Freiham* is currently[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] under development.

## History and description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History and description")]

### Aubing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Aubing")]

Main article: [History of Aubing](/wiki/History_of_Aubing "History of Aubing")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Aubinger_Lohe_1a.jpg/250px-Aubinger_Lohe_1a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Aubinger_Lohe_1a.jpg)

Aubinger Lohe forest

Aubing was first documented in 1010 AD as Ubingen. This name was most likely derived from the name Ubo of the [Bavarii](/wiki/Bavarii "Bavarii"), however no such person with that name is actually known of. The longer story which most school children are told, is of "Founder Ubo, who settled here with his cart" is just as well established as the neighbouring borough of [Pasing](/wiki/Pasing "Pasing")'s founding story, in that its name stemmed from "Paoso". Neither are accurate.

In 1818 the municipality of Aubing was established, with the districts Aubing, Freiham and Moosschwaige. On 1 April 1942 the Aubing municipality was connected to Munich, but without a treaty of incorporation.

Aubing is divided by the Munich-Buchloe [railway](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") line in Alt-Aubing and Neu-Aubing.

Alt-Aubing reflects the origins of Aubing with buildings that still stand today. The old village centre is built around the gothic/baroque parish church St. Quirin with its approximately one-thousand-year-old [romanesque](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") tower built in 1480.

Neuaubing arose at the start of the 20th century as settlement for railwaymen and workers.

#### Coat of arms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Coat of arms")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Wappen-Aubing.png/250px-Wappen-Aubing.png)](/wiki/File:Wappen-Aubing.png)

Coat of Arms of Aubing

The Aubing [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") goes back to a local aristocratic family from the 12th to the 14th century. It was first mentioned in a document of a known Hartmann of Aubing on 1 May 1334. The layout goes back to [Otto Hupp](/wiki/Otto_Hupp "Otto Hupp"). This was assessment was approved of on 23 February 1933. The coat of arms shows 3 leaves on a silver background.

Today like all other Munich districts, Aubing carries the Munich coat of arms.

#### Clubs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Clubs")]

Alt-Aubing has many clubs and associations, one of which is the Burschenverein Aubing (Aubing Gentleman's Club) that sets up the maypole in the village centre every three years. Many figures are carved into the wood of the maypole.

### Lochhausen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lochhausen")]

Lochhausen was first documented in 948/955 AD as Lohhusa. The name means house or houses in the forest.

In 1818 Lochhausen became part of the Langwied municipality. On 1 April 1942 this municipality then became part attached to the city of Munich.

The old village centre is formed around the parish church of [St. Michael](/wiki/Michael_(archangel) "Michael (archangel)").

### Langwied

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Langwied")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Langwieder_See_Badezugang.JPG/250px-Langwieder_See_Badezugang.JPG)](/wiki/File:Langwieder_See_Badezugang.JPG)

[Langwieder See](/wiki/Langwieder_lake_district "Langwieder lake district")

Langwied was first document in 1269/1271 as "Lanquat". The name Langwied means *am langen Wald* (althochdeutsch: daz witu = wood, forest). The ending "quat" used up to the 15th century could come from *waten* (the verb "to wade"), and could allude to a fjord for wading through of the Langwieder brook.

In 1818 the Langwied municipality was formed with Lochhausen and [Gröbenzell](/wiki/Gr%C3%B6benzell "Gröbenzell"). On 1 April 1942 the municipality was incorporated into the city of Munich by treaty from 29 August/2 September 1941. Northern parts of Langwied formed the Gröbenzell municipality in 1952 along with other parts of municipalities.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Transport")]

Further information: [Freihamer Allee](/wiki/Freihamer_Allee "Freihamer Allee")

### Car

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Car")]

The area in and directly around Aubing, Lochhausen and Langwied is connected to by the following junctions:

[A 99](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_99 "Bundesautobahn 99")

* Junction 5 München-Freiham-Mitte
* Junction 7 München-Lochhausen
* Junction 8 Kreuz München-West

[A 8](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_8 "Bundesautobahn 8")

* Junction 80 München-Langwied
* Junction 81 Kreuz München-West

### Public Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Public Transport")]

By [Munich S-Bahn](/wiki/Munich_S-Bahn "Munich S-Bahn"), the district is bound by the S4, S5, S6 and S8 lines, at the stops and stations of [Langwied](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen-Langwied_railway_station "München-Langwied railway station"), [Lochhausen](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen-Lochhausen_railway_station "München-Lochhausen railway station"), [Leienfelsstraße](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen-Leienfelsstra%C3%9Fe_railway_station "München-Leienfelsstraße railway station"), [Aubing](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen-Leienfelsstra%C3%9Fe_railway_station "München-Leienfelsstraße railway station"), [Westkreuz](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchen-Westkreuz_railway_station "München-Westkreuz railway station") and [Neuaubing](/w/index.php?title=M%C3%BCnchen-Neuaubing_railway_station&action=edit&redlink=1 "München-Neuaubing railway station (page does not exist)") of the [MVV](/wiki/M%C3%BCnchner_Verkehrs-_und_Tarifverbund "Münchner Verkehrs- und Tarifverbund"). Numerous bus lines further connect the district.

### Longest tunnel in Bavaria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Aubing-Lochhausen-Langwied&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Longest tunnel in Bavaria")]

Since the opening of the section of the A 99 as a connection between the München-Lochhausen junction and the [A 96](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_96 "Bundesautobahn 96") München-Lindau route in February 2006 which resulted in the tunneling of large parts of Aubing, at 1,935m long Aubing has the largest automobile tunnel in Bavaria.
